topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed envelope felt alien in my hands, the paper thin as old parchment. Inside, a formal, spidery script detailed a resignation, dated from thirty years prior. My father’s handwriting swam before my eyes, elegant and detached. I remember the precise moment I unfolded it—a sudden, sharp clench in my chest, a tightening around my jaw that made my teeth ache. It wasn't just the words; it was the implication, the casual dismissal of everything we’d built. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound a faint rustle in the quiet study. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, my hands balling into fists until the knuckles were white. "What kind of nonsense is this?" I muttered, the sound tight and brittle. The air in the room suddenly felt too thin, too cold.

[story 2]

I found the letter tucked inside a forgotten box of old tax documents. It was my mother’s, written in her familiar, looping script, announcing her departure from her career. Reading the final sentence—a neat, professional sign-off—sent a wave of profound irritation through me. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that didn't seem to reach my lungs properly. I dropped the letter onto the desk with a deliberate, hard thud. I stared at it, my gaze fixed on the ink, trying to decipher a hidden malice I couldn't locate. I pushed my chair back violently, the scrape of wood against the floor echoing loudly. "Unbelievable," I whispered, the word laced with a bitterness that tasted metallic on my tongue. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, letting the frustration simmer beneath my skin like an unquenchable fever.

[story 3]

The letter arrived slipped under the door of my apartment, a relic from my grandfather’s past. It was my grandmother’s resignation, penned in a tone of weary finality. When I finally opened it, the paper seemed to vibrate with disapproval. My shoulders immediately tensed, drawing up toward my ears as if bracing for impact. I read the carefully chosen vocabulary, each word a deliberate slight against the memory of her dedication. A slow, simmering heat began to spread from my stomach outward. I didn't move for a long time, just sat on the edge of the sofa, my hands gripping the worn fabric until my fingers cramped. When I finally spoke, my voice was low, dangerously level. "After all this time," I managed, the inflection sharp enough to cut glass, "this is how you leave things?"
